coredata.dc:title: Homochiral fluorescence responsive molecularly imprinted polymer: Highly chiral enantiomer resolution and quantitative detection of L-penicillamine
coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Yan
Wang, Hai-Yan
He, Xi-Wen
Li, Wen-You
Zhang, Yu-Kui
coredata.dc:description: In this work, we innovatively synthesized homochiral fluorescence nano molecularly imprinted polymers (D-MIP) with dual affinity (metal ion affinity and homochiral affinity) for the specific separation and detection of L-penicillamine (L-PA), which is a core-shell structure with a SiO2-covered CDs core and an imprinted layer with L-PA cavities. A switch for fluorescence response was built by chelating grafted Cu2+, what's more, the imprinted L-PA was pre immobilized by Cu2+ to form the directional imprinting with predetermined spatial structure. More importantly, the homochiral affinity of D-galactose in homochiral molecularly imprinted polymers (D-MIP) greatly enhanced the selective adsorption of L-PA, and D-MIP showed a high selectivity factor (α) of 3.45, which is 1.9 times that of the non-homochiral molecularly imprinted polymers (MIP). Meanwhile, D-MIP exhibited a high enantiomeric excess (ee) value of 56% for separation of racemic PA. Additionally, a high sensitive and selective method was established for the detection of L-PA.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Homochiral
Molecularly imprinted polymer
Chiral enantiomer resolution
L-penicillamine detection

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Detection of L-PA in lake water and human urine
Preparation and structural characterization of the D-MIP
Optimization of the reaction conditions for synthesis of the D-MIP
Feasibility of enantioselective recognition and chiral resolution
Verification of highly chiral enantiomer resolution of D-MIP
The quantitative detection of L-PA based on D-MIP
Detection of L-PA in lake water and human urine

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Key Research and Development Program of China

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM image of CDs (Inset: HRTEM image) (A), CDs@SiO2 (B) and D-MIP (C). (D) EDS elemental mapping of D-MIP by TEM. (E) EDS energy spectra of D-MIP by TEM. Scale bar: 50 nm.
FT-IR spectra (A), XRD analysis (B), Zeta potential (C) and TGA analysis (D) of nanoparticles.

Excitation wavelength-independent fluorescence spectra of CDs (A) and D-MIP (B). (C) Fluorescence spectra of CDs, CDs@SiO2, D-MIP and D-NIP. (D) UV–vis absorption spectrum, fluorescence excitation spectrum and emission spectrum of D-MIP. Inset: Photographs under natural light (left) and 365 nm UV lamp irradiation (right) of D-MIP. (E) The photostability of the D-MIP and D-MIP+L-PA. (F) The CD spectra of D-MIP and D-galactose.

(A) The fluorescence response of D-MIP upon addition of L-PA at various concentrations (0–140 μM). Inset: The Stern-Volmer plots of D-MIP. (B) The fluorescence response of D-NIP upon addition of L-PA at various concentrations (0–140 μM). Inset: The Stern-Volmer plots of D-NIP. (C) The fluorescence response of D-MIP upon addition of D-PA at various concentrations (0–140 μM). Inset: The Stern-Volmer plots of D-MIP. Experimental conditions: the concentrations of all the polymers are 0.5 mg/mL, V = 1.0 mL, pH = 7.0 (PBS, 10 mM), Ex = 520 nm.

(A) The CD spectra of L-PA adsorption on different polymers. (B) The CD spectra of racemic mixture of D-PA and L-PA adsorbed on D-MIP and D-NIP. (C) CD spectra of mixed D- and L-PA aqueous solution in different volume ratio (D:L); (D) The linear fits of CD signals at 227 nm in different ee%.
Illustration of the preparation of the D-MIP (A) and highly chiral enantiomer resolution and quantitative detection of L-penicillamine based on D-MIP (B).
Analytical results for the detection of L-PA in lake water and human urine samples.

head.ce:title: Homochiral fluorescence responsive molecularly imprinted polymer: Highly chiral enantiomer resolution and quantitative detection of L-penicillamine

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry, Research Center for Analytical Sciences, State Key Laboratory of Medicinal Chemical Biology, Tianjin Key Laboratory of Biosensing and Molecular Recognition, Nankai University, Tianjin 300071, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry, Research Center for Analytical Sciences, State Key Laboratory of Medicinal Chemical Biology, Tianjin Key Laboratory of Biosensing and Molecular Recognition, Nankai University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Chemistry, Research Center for Analytical Sciences, State Key Laboratory of Medicinal Chemical Biology, Tianjin Key Laboratory of Biosensing and Molecular Recognition, Nankai University, Tianjin 300071, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: National Chromatographic Research and Analysis Center, Dalian Institute of Chemical Physics, Chinese Academy of Sciences, Dalian 116023, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: National Chromatographic Research and Analysis Center, Dalian Institute of Chemical Physics, Chinese Academy of Sciences

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: National Chromatographic Research and Analysis Center, Dalian Institute of Chemical Physics, Chinese Academy of Sciences, Dalian 116023, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this work, we innovatively synthesized homochiral fluorescence nano molecularly imprinted polymers (D-MIP) with dual affinity (metal ion affinity and homochiral affinity) for the specific separation and detection of L-penicillamine (L-PA), which is a core-shell structure with a SiO2-covered CDs core and an imprinted layer with L-PA cavities. A switch for fluorescence response was built by chelating grafted Cu2+, what's more, the imprinted L-PA was pre immobilized by Cu2+ to form the directional imprinting with predetermined spatial structure. More importantly, the homochiral affinity of D-galactose in homochiral molecularly imprinted polymers (D-MIP) greatly enhanced the selective adsorption of L-PA, and D-MIP showed a high selectivity factor (α) of 3.45, which is 1.9 times that of the non-homochiral molecularly imprinted polymers (MIP). Meanwhile, D-MIP exhibited a high enantiomeric excess (ee) value of 56% for separation of racemic PA. Additionally, a high sensitive and selective method was established for the detection of L-PA.

ce:section.ce:para: Chirality is one of the major phenomena in nature, and resolution of a racemic mixture of chiral substances is a great significance in pharmaceutics, agriculture and food additive industry (Hu et al., 2020a, 2020b; Li et al., 2019). Generally, the properties of a pair of enantiomers are of great difference, one of them shows a satisfactory pharmacological activity and biological metabolism behavior, while the other may have side effects and even toxicity (Alatawi et al., 2018; Bukhari et al., 2019; Gao et al., 2016). Penicillamine (PA) is the metabolite of penicillin that chelates to most heavy metal ions (Huang et al., 2018; Ma et al., 2019; Pawar et al., 2015; Yu et al., 2018). PA exists as D - or L- enantiomers with different properties of biological and toxicological. D-PA is used clinically for the treatment of chronic active hepatitis, rheumatoid arthritis, metal poisoning and other immune diseases (Mohd Zawawi, 2020; Weiss et al., 2011). However, L-PA has toxic and side effects on organisms to a certain extent, which can lead to bone marrow damage, olfactory and visual deterioration, even can lead to cancer, etc (Durán et al., 2017; Ma et al., 2019; Ngamdee et al., 2015). Moreover, L-PA can also pollute the water resources. Therefore, it is indispensable to establish a sensitive and efficient method for the resolution and detection of PA enantiomers.

Molecular imprinting technology (MIT) refers to the copolymerization of template molecules and functional monomers with covalent or non-covalent binding force under the action of cross-linking agents to prepare polymers (Fang and Li, 2007; Wu et al., 2020). Molecular imprinting polymer (MIP) has the advantages of precognition, specificity and practicality (Bi and Liu, 2014; Gu et al., 2010; Plausinaitis et al., 2020) Therefore, MIP has been widely used in many fields such as selective reagent in liquid chromatography (Baltrons et al., 2013; He et al., 2014; Xu et al., 2010), artificial antibodies (Cakir et al., 2013; Poma et al., 2013; Zangiabadi and Zhao, 2020), and sensing (Ahmad et al., 2015; Gui et al., 2018; Viter et al., 2019). By establishing specific molecular recognition sites, the enantioselective recognition and enrichment of the target enantiomer molecule can be realized, and the introduction of a molecular imprinting technique into resolution of chiral enantiomers was reported (Alatawi et al., 2018; Liu et al., 2020; Su et al., 2011).

At the same time, homochiral factors are promising candidates for chiral separation. Homochiral porous polymers, which contain numerous asymmetric centers, are capable of stereoselective sorption and chiral resolution of racemic mixtures (Dybtsev et al., 2007; Lu et al., 2019). However, as far as we know, homochiral molecularly imprinted polymer for chiral separation has not been reported, so we innovatively combined molecular imprinting technology with homochiral factors to synthesize homochiral molecularly imprinted polymer for chiral enantiomer resolution.

Until now, a variety of methods for the determination of PA have been reported, including electrochemistry, spectrophotometric technique, high performance liquid chromatography and flow injection (Pawar et al., 2015; Saracino et al., 2013; Suliman, 2003; Torriero et al., 2007; Zhai et al., 2015). However, some of these methods can not be used well in practical application due to time-consuming, expensive equipment and complex sample pretreatment. In recent years, carbon dots (CDs) have been widely applied in sensing (Li et al., 2016; Zhang et al., 2018), bioimaging (Zheng et al., 2015) and photocatalysis (Wang et al., 2013) because of its high sensitivity, simple operation and economic advantages. As we all known, some metal ions, especially heavy metal ions such as Cu2+, Hg2+, are often used as quenching agents, meanwhile, PA has stronger chelating ability with metal ions, so it can be used to build a probe to detect L-PA by restoring the fluorescence of CDs.

Herein, considering the adverse toxicity and ecological effects of inactive L-PA enantiomers on human body, we have innovatively synthesized a new nano material (D-MIP) by metal ions affinity, homochiral affinity and surface imprinting for the selective separation and enrichment of L-PA to achieve enantiomer resolution and quantitative detection of L-PA ( Scheme 1). The surface imprinting layer and L-PA affinity sites were prepared through metal chelation and hydrogen bonding, more importantly, homochiral affinity of D-galactose enhanced the homochiral adsorption of enantiomer L-PA, and the selectivity factor (α) of D-MIP is 1.9 times that of the non-homochiral molecularly imprinted polymers (MIP). Notably, the D-MIP exhibited an ee value up to 56% for separation of racemic PA, showing an excellent chiral separation ability. Additionally, L-PA can increase the fluorescence intensity of D-MIP by blocking the photoinduced electron transfer (PET) effect between Cu2+ and CDs, thus a high sensitive and selective method was established for the detection of L-PA. These results provide a new way to explore the application of homochiral molecularly imprinted polymer in high performance chiral resolution and detection.

Tetraethyl orthosilicate (TEOS), dioctyl sulfosuccinate sodium salt (AOT), CuSO4.5H2O, acrylamide (AM) and N,N,N′,N′-tetramethylethylenediamine (TEMED) were purchased from J&K Scientific Ltd (Beijing, China). NaOH, formamid, Brij-30, NH3·H2O, ethanol (EtOH), L-penicillamine (L-PA) and D-penicillamine (D-PA) were bought from Aladdin Biochemical Technology Co. Ltd (Shanghai, China). Citric Acid and urea were provided by Damao Chemical Reagent Factory (Tianjin, China). Iminodiacetic acid (IDA) and glycidyloxypropyltrimethoxysilane (GLYMO) were purchased from Shanghai Dibai Biotechnology Co. Ltd (Shanghai, China). Hexyl hydride was obtained from Concord Technologies Co. Ltd (Tianjin, China). D-galactose and ammonium persulphate (APS) were purchased from Macklin Biochemical Technology Co. Ltd (Shanghai, China). There was no additional purification of all reagents.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) was performed using the HITACHI HT7700 Exalens transmission electron microscope (JEOL, Japan) at an accelerating voltage of 200 kV. High resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) was performed using the JEM-2800 (JEOL, Japan). Scanning electron microscopy (SEM) images and energy dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDS) chemical mapping were obtained from scanning electron microscope (Shimadzu SS-550, JEOL, Japan). The X-ray diffraction (XRD) were collected using a D8 ADVANCE X-ray diffractometer (Bruker AXS, German). The FT-IR spectrum was obtained using a TENSOR II FT-IR spectrophotometer (Bruker, Germany). The fluorescence spectra were collected on a fluorescence spectrophotometer (F-4500, Hitachi, Japan). The fluorescence decay curves excited at 520 nm were measured by an FLS-920 spectrophotometer (Edinburgh, UK). The UV–visible (UV–vis) absorption spectra were collected using the UV-2450 spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Japan). The circular dichroism (CD) spectra were recorded on a circular dichroism spectrometer (MOS-450, BioLogic, France).

Carbon dots (CDs) are prepared according to the previously reported literature (Zhu et al., 2018) with slight changes. 1.0 g of citric acid and 2.0 g of urea were dissolved in 10.0 mL of formamide, and then transferred into a Teflon-lined stainless and heated at 180 ℃ for 8 h to obtain the dark brown solution. The CDs were purified by alkali washing. After the product was cooled down to room temperature, 2 mL of the obtained solution was taken out and mixed with 4 mL NaOH solution (50 mg/mL) and stirred at room temperature for 15 min. The resultant mixture was centrifuged (13,000 rpm) by 10 min to remove any large or agglomerated particles, and then the precipitate was dissolved in 5 mL of ultrapure water. Finally, the CDs solution was stored in a refrigerator at 4 ℃.

CDs were encapsulated in silica nanoparticles based on the reverse microemulsion polymerization (Zhang et al., 2020). First, 0.8 g of AOT and 1.55 mL of Brij-30 were added to 100 mL round bottom flask, then 20 mL of n-hexane was added to the above solution and stirred at room temperature for 15 min until the mixed solution was transparent. Subsequently, 100 μL of NH3·H2O was added to the 700 μL of the CDs solution and mixed uniformly, then the mixed solution was added dropwise with syringe to the above transparent solution containing AOT, Brij-30 and n-hexane. After the microemulsion was formed by stirring at room temperature for 1 h, 500 μL of TEOS was added to the microemulsion, then reacted at room temperature for 5 h. Finally, demulsification was carried out with 40 mL of EtOH. The product was centrifuged (13,000 rpm) by 10 min and washed three times with EtOH and water, respectively.

To introduce IDA on the surface of CDs@SiO2, GLYMO was reacted with IDA to form GLYMO-IDA according to the previous report with minor modifications (Li et al., 2015). Briefly, 4.25 g IDA was dissolved in 50 mL ultrapure water, and the resultant solution was adjusted to pH 11 with 10 M NaOH. Then, the solution was placed in an ice-bath at 0 ℃, followed by adding 1.4 mL of GLYMO slowly (in 40 min) under stirring. The resultant solution was heated to 65 ℃ for reaction 6 h under stirring. Afterward, the reaction round-bottom flask was placed in an ice-bath for 10 min to lower the temperature to 0 ℃, and 1.6 mL GLYMO was added slowly again and reacted at 65 ℃ for another 6 h. Subsequently, the reaction solution temperature was decreased to 0 ℃, then 1.7 mL GLYMO was added and reacted at 65 ℃ for 6 h. Finally, the resultant solution was adjusted to pH 6 with concentrated HCl, and GLYMO-IDA was obtained. CDs@SiO2 @IDA was prepared as follows: 500 mg CDs@SiO2 was dispersed in 20 mL of GLYMO-IDA with ultrasonication vibration for 10 min. Then the reaction was performed at 55 ℃ with mechanically stirring for 36 h. CDs@SiO2 @IDA was obtained after three times washing with water and vacuum dried at 25 ℃. Then, 50 mg of CDs@SiO2 @IDA was dispersed in 1 mL of 30 mg/mL CuSO4 solution, and kept shaking for 2 h at room temperature to introduce Cu2+, after washed three times with water, CDs@SiO2 @IDA-Cu2+ was obtained and then vacuum dried at 25 ℃.

50 mg of CDs@SiO2@IDA-Cu2+, 40 mg of zinc acrylate (ZnA), 20 mg of acrylamide (AM), 30 mg of L-PA and 30 mg of D-galactose were dissolved in 15 mL ultrapure water and stirred under argon atmosphere at 65 ℃ for 1 h. Then, 100 μL of TEMED was added and the mixture was stirred for another 30 min. Next, 30 mg of APS dissolved in 5 mL of ultrapure water was injected, and the mixture was reacted at 65 ℃ for 4 h. After 4 h, the product was centrifuged and eluted 10 times with MeOH. The D-galactose was adsorbed through the specific binding site on the MIP. 30 mg of MIP was equilibrated in 25 mL of D-galactose solution (25 mg/mL) for 3 h at room temperature on the shaker. Finally, the D-MIP was washed three times with ultrapure water and then lyophilized.

In contrast, the non-imprinted polymers (D-NIP) was prepared in the same manner as that of D-MIP, but in the absence of L-PA. In the meantime, MIP was prepared, the remaining steps were the same as the D-MIP except that D-galactose was not rebound.

All the fluorescence experiments were carried out using the following conditions: the excitation wavelength was set as 520 nm, both slit widths of the excitation and emission were set as 10 nm and the photomultiplier tube voltage was set at 700 V. The D-MIP or D-NIP (0.5 mL, 1 mg/mL) was added to a 2 mL colorimetric tube, then the different concentration solution (20–140 μM) of L-PA or D-PA was added in succession, and the mixed solution was diluted to 1 mL with PBS buffer solution (pH 7.0).

In order to verify the highly chiral enantiomer resolution of D-MIP, the circular dichroism spectrum was scanned. In the experiment, the mixed solutions containing 0.5 mM L-PA and 1 mg/mL D-MIP were incubated on a shaker at room temperature for 2 h, then centrifuged (13,000 rpm) by 10 min, and the supernatant was scanned by circular dichroism. In another experiment, the mixed solutions containing 1 mg/mL D-MIP or D-NIP and racemic mixtures consisting of 50% L-PA and 50% D-PA with a constant total concentration of 0.5 mM were incubated on a shaker at room temperature for 2 h, then centrifuged (13,000 rpm) by 10 min, and the supernatant spectrum was collected by circular dichroism.

The quantitative calculation of ee value was carried out by experiment that L-PA and D-PA were mixed in a volume ratio of 10:0, 9:1, 8:2, 7:3, 6:4, 5:5, 4:6, 3:7, 2:8, 1:9, and 0:10 and then the CD signals of those mixed samples were recorded.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of L-PA in lake water and human urine
ce:section.ce:para: In order to demonstrate the practical utility of the new assay, lake water and human urine were used as real samples. The lake water samples were obtained from the Xinkai Lake in the campus of Nankai University and pretreated by centrifugation and 0.45 µM filter membrane to remove the suspended matter and other impurities. Human urine samples were taken from adult female volunteer in the laboratory and pretreated by centrifugation and 0.45 µM filter membrane to remove the solid suspensions and other impurities. Then the urine samples were diluted 10 times with PBS buffer solution (pH 7.0). After original L-PA of lake water and human urine was measured, exogenous L-PA at different concentrations was added and detected using the new assay.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation and structural characterization of the D-MIP
ce:section.ce:para: The complete synthesis process of the D-MIP via metal chelating imprinting and the application process of chiral enantiomer resolution and target detection are shown in Scheme 1. This method mainly involves synthesis of CDs, reverse microemulsion method for silicon coating, grafting ligands on the surface of CDs@SiO2, chelating metal ions, rebinding of homochiral D-galactose and L-PA template molecules, surface imprinting by metal chelation and hydrogen bonding, and rebinding D-galactose. Firstly, CDs which are regarded as fluorescent response signal were synthesized as in the experimental section, showing good dispersion and spherical appearance. As shown in Fig. 1A, the average diameter of CDs is 3.3 nm and the well-resolved (002) lattice spacing was determined by HRTEM to be 0.31 nm, proving excellent crystallinity of the synthesized CDs. Secondly, in order to synthesize controllable silica particles with uniform size, reverse microemulsion method was adopted to synthesize size-adjustable silica nanoparticles with the diameter of 44 nm as is demonstrated in Fig. 1B. Subsequently, GLYMO-IDA was grafted on the surface of CDs@SiO2 through silane coupling reaction, the negatively charged carboxyl group was anchored on the surface, and then chelated with positively charged Cu2+ (Wang et al., 2019). Next, L-PA was immobilized on the CDs@SiO2@IDA-Cu2+ surface by Cu2+ coordination affinity. Then, the surface imprinting layer was formed though metal chelation and hydrogen bonding. Finally, the imprinted template molecules were eluted and then the homochiral D-galactose was rebound, and thus the molecularly imprinted polymer D-MIP was synthesized. As is illustrated in Fig. 1C, the imprinted polymer layer can be clearly observed on the surface of the nanoparticles, and the well-scattered D-MIP nanoparticles with regular sphericity were about 51 nm according to the TEM image, which proved that the imprinted polymer was successfully synthesized. Similarly, D-NIP also presents a sphere appearance with the size of about 48 nm (Fig. S1A).

In addition, the DLS results show that the average hydrodynamic diameters of CDs, CDs@SiO2, CDs@SiO2@IDA-Cu2+ and D-MIP are 11.5 nm, 122.9 nm, 134.4 nm and 166.8 nm, respectively (Fig. S2). The increasing particle size also indicate the successful formation of molecularly imprinted polymer. Scanning electron microscopy (SEM) was further used to characterize the morphology of CDs@SiO2, D-NIP and D-MIP (Fig. S1B, C and D), which showed that they all had uniform spherical structure. The EDS elemental mapping analysis by SEM (Fig. S1E), EDS elemental mapping analysis by TEM (Fig. 1D) and EDS energy spectra (Fig. 1E) showed that D-MIP nanoparticles were mainly composed of C, N, O, Si, and Cu, which also provided strong evidence for the successful grafting of Cu2+.

The surface characteristics of these nanoparticles were further verified by FT-IR spectroscopy ( Fig. 2A). The peaks of 1569 cm−1 can be observed due to the stretching vibration of COO− of the CDs, and the signals at 1053 and 787 cm−1 were ascribed to the stretching vibration of Si−O−Si and Si−C, respectively, which indicate SiO2 was successfully coated. The peak at 1400 cm−1 could be assigned to the characteristic absorption of C−O of IDA framework, indicating the successful grafting of IDA onto the CDs@SiO2 surface. Furthermore, the absorption peak of the D-MIP at 1589 cm−1 was attributed to the −COO− stretching vibration. Obviously, the absorbance peak of the D-MIP at 1350 cm−1 may be caused by the C−CH3 bending vibration of residual templates. The results of FT-IR confirmed the deposition of surface imprinted layer on the surface of CDs@SiO2. XRD curves were shown in Fig. 2B. After CDs was coated with SiO2, the crystal structure changed from carbon to silicon structure, meanwhile, CDs@SiO2, CDs@SiO2 @IDA-Cu2+, D-MIP and D-NIP showed a typical SiO2 crystal structure according to JCPDS card of SiO2 (no. 01–0438), showing that the polymerization reaction was mild and did not change the original crystal structure of CDs@SiO2.

Moreover, the zeta potential was measured to verify the successful synthesis of each step (Fig. 2C). CDs presented the negative zeta potential (−0.11 mV) due to the surface −COO− with negative charge. And the zeta potential of the CDs@SiO2 and CDs@SiO2@IDA decreased gradually to −37.97 mV and −44.43 mV, respectively, which is due to the surface Si−OH and −COO− with negative charge. The zeta potential of CDs@SiO2@IDA-Cu2+ is +0.15 mV, but that of D-MIP changes to −1.01 mV, which can also indicate the successful synthesis of D-MIP. Further characterization of the D-MIP was achieved using thermogravimetric analysis (TGA, Fig. 2D). The weight loss of CDs@SiO2 @IDA-Cu2+ is higher than that of CDs@SiO2 about 3%, which is due to the IDA grafting and chelation with Cu2+. TGA analysis of the D-MIP is similar to CDs@SiO2 and CDs@SiO2@IDA-Cu2+ at 270–800 ℃, therefore, the weight loss is primarily attribute to the thermal degradation of the SiO2 cores. And the weight loss of the D-MIP is about 15% higher than that of CDs@SiO2@IDA-Cu2+, which is consistent with the test results mentioned above, further illustrating the successful preparation of D-MIP.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimization of the reaction conditions for synthesis of the D-MIP
ce:section.ce:para: The amounts of two monomers (ZnA and AM), crosslinker and accelerators (TEMED), CuSO4 and D-galactose were optimized to achieve higher IF. The template was immobilized with monomers by metal chelation and hydrogen bonding, therefore the D-MIP can generate more L-PA imprinted sites and enhance the ability of specific recognition. Considering the specificity of D-MIP, the optimal conditions were 30 mg of zinc acrylate, 20 mg of acrylamide, 100 μL of TEMED and 30 mg/mL of CuSO4 (Fig. S3). The amount of D-galactose is important for improving the ability of specific adsorption and enantiomer resolution. The IF and α both increased first and then decreased as the concentration of D-galactose increased from 20 to 40 mg/mL (Fig. S4). Considering the two factors of IF and α, the compromise concentration of D-galactose was chosen as 25 mg/mL.

CDs exhibited excitation-wavelength-independent emission property, in other words, the emission wavelength does not change with the excitation wavelength within the range of 480–560 nm ( Fig. 3A). As demonstrated in Fig. 3B, D-MIP also shows the property of excitation independent emission, implying that the synthesis of imprinted polymers does not change the fluorescence properties of CDs. In addition, the emission peak is intact when the excitation wavelength is 520 nm, so we choose the excitation wavelength of 520 nm for fluorescence determination. The fluorescence emission spectra of different materials are shown in the Fig. 3C. The fluorescence of CDs coated silica shifted blue, but after imprinting polymerization, the fluorescence shifted to the original position. Moreover, the fluorescence intensity of D-MIP was higher than that of D-NIP may be due to the existence of residual templates.

The lavender solution of D-MIP with strong red fluorescence under UV radiation (365 nm) was obtained (inset in Fig. 3D). The maximum excitation of D-MIP is located at 562 nm, which is almost the same as the absorption peak, indicating that there is almost no radiation-free transition of D-MIP. As demonstrated in Fig. 3E, the fluorescence intensity of D-MIP in the presence of L-PA is significantly enhanced compared with the initial fluorescence intensity of D-MIP. The fluorescence enhancement can be attributed to the specific recognition of the L-PA by the oriented imprinted cavities. And their photostability were investigated by continuous irradiation with a beam of 520 nm UV light in aqueous solution. As shown in Fig. 3E, after 60 min of irradiation, the fluorescence intensity changed negligibly and showed good photostability. Circular dichroism (CD) spectroscopy was carried out to verify the homochirality of D-MIP. As shown in Fig. 3F, D-MIP has an absorption peak at 190 nm same as D-galactose in the CD spectrum, demonstrating the homochirality of the D-MIP.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Feasibility of enantioselective recognition and chiral resolution
ce:section.ce:para:
Due to the nanostructure and chiral properties of the prepared D-MIP, we constructed a method based on fluorescent response of D-MIP to recognize penicillamine enantiomers and specific adsorption of molecularly imprinted cavities for enantiomeric separation. The fluorescent response of these nanoparticles for L-PA and D-PA was studied in 10 mM PBS solution containing 0.5 mg/mL nanoparticles at the excitation wavelength of 520 nm. L-PA had an enhancement effect on the fluorescence of the D-MIP and D-NIP, and the (F–F0)/F0 was used as the index of fluorescence enhancement capacity, where F0 and F were the fluorescence intensities of D-MIP or D-NIP in the absence and presence of L-PA, respectively. Selectivity factor (α) was calculated according to the ratio ofenhancement amount of L-PA captured by D-MIP over that of D-PA, i.e. α=KD‐MIP/L‐PA / KD-MIP/
D-PA . K is the fluorescence enhancement constant which was calculated from the equation of (F–F0)/F0 = K[P], and [P] was the concentration of L-PA.

As displayed in Fig. 4, the fluorescence intensity of D-MIP and D-NIP enhanced linearly with the increase of L-PA concentration. It was seen that the enhancement of fluorescent intensity of the D-MIP (Fig. 4A) is much larger than that of D-NIP (Fig. 4B) at the same concentration of L-PA. Imprinting factor (IF) was used to evaluate the imprinting effect which can be calculated by the formula: IF=KD-MIP/L-PA / KD-NIP/L-PA. By calculation, IF is 4.34, which indicates that D-MIP hads good imprinting effect to L-PA. Generally, the changes of fluorescent intensity are related with the binding affinity interactions between the imprinted sites and the target. Because molecular imprinting technology can specifically select L-PA as template molecule, the molecularly imprinted material retained the configuration in size and shape, so the chiral molecule L-PA was specifically recognized. However, D-NIP does not have excellent specific response due to the lack of imprinted sites. In addition, the fluorescence enhancement of D-MIP for L-PA is much greater than that for D-PA (Fig. 4C), which is because metal ions can coordinate with the sulfhydryl group on the surface of L-PA and the directional imprinted cavities formed by copper and L-PA have high specific adsorption ability to L-PA. What's more, the presence of homochiral D-galactose also increased the homochiral selective adsorption of L-PA. So, the existence of metal ions affinity, directional imprinting effect and homochiral affinity determine that L-PA can be selectively captured by D-MIP. Thus, D-MIP is the highly efficient adsorbent for L-PA and enantiomeric resolution agent. The imprinted polymers without D-galactose adsorption (MIP) and with L-galactose adsorption (L-MIP) were also prepared for comparison. As shown in Fig. S5, the fluorescence response of MIP and L-MIP to L-PA and D-PA is weaker than that of D-MIP. Through calculation, the selection factors of MIP and L-MIP to L-PA are 1.82 and 1.57, respectively, which are lower than that of D-MIP, verifying the high enantioselectivity of D-MIP. At the same time, the fluorescence response of D-MIP to D-galactose was studied (Fig. S6). The results showed that the fluorescence change of D-MIP to D-galactose is negligible, indicating that D-galactose did not affect the fluorescence intensity of the entire system.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Verification of highly chiral enantiomer resolution of D-MIP
ce:section.ce:para: Circular dichroism (CD) measurements were used to confirm the chiral resolution ability of D-MIP ( Fig. 5). The adsorption capacity of different imprinted polymers for L-PA is obviously different, as is illustrated in Fig. 5A, both D-NIP and MIP have a certain degree of adsorption of L-PA, which is attributed to nonspecific adsorption and specific adsorption without chiral affinity. Compared with the original concentration of L-PA, the circular dichroism signal of L-PA was reduced by only 3.3 after D-NIP treatment. However, the circular dichroism signal of L-PA was reduced by 19.1 after D-MIP treatment. Compared with D-MIP with chiral affinity and specific adsorption, the adsorption capacity of L-PA by D-NIP was very small. It is proved that D-MIP has high selectivity for L-PA adsorption. The prepared imprinting materials D-MIP and D-NIP were used for the selective binding with L-PA in racemic mixtures consisting of 50% L-PA and 50% D-PA with a constant total concentration of 0.5 mM. As shown in Fig. 5B, the racemic solution treated by D-MIP shows the circular dichroism peak of D-PA, corresponding to the excess of D-PA. This indicated that D-MIP have stronger interaction with L-PA, leaving more D-PA in the solution. So, the solution showed a negative signal, while D-NIP only shows a slight change.

In order to quantitatively evaluate the enantiomer selectivity, the ee value was determined by circular dichroism. We prepared a series of solutions with the same concentration (0.5 mM) but different volume ratio (Fig. 5C). The volume ratio was converted into ee% according to the equation: ee%= |nD – nL|/(nD + nL) × 100% (Li et al., 2020; Zhuo et al., 2018). According to the fitting line (Fig. 5D), the intensity of D-PA excess signal (−20.4) corresponding to the ee value is 56.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The quantitative detection of L-PA based on D-MIP
ce:section.ce:para: Interestingly, the fluorescence intensity of D-MIP was gradually enhanced by L-PA (see Fig. 4A), and the fluorescence intensity ratio (F–F0)/F0 displayed good linearity with concentration in the range from 20 to 140 μM. The linear equation is (F–F0)/F0 = 0.01098c – 0.02464 (R2 = 0.995, c represents the concentration of L-PA). The limit of detection (LOD) for L-PA is as low as 117 nM (LOD = 3Sd/s). As a result, the D-MIP has a potential to be developed as a fluorescence sensor for the detection of L-PA. As shown in Table S1, compared with the previously reported method, the LOD and linear range of the proposed method are comparable or even better.

The mechanism of fluorescence enhancement was discussed by theoretical support and experiments. Due to the grafting of Cu2+ into D-MIP, the photoinduced electron transfer (PET) was occurred between Cu2+ and CDs, which leads to the fluorescence quenching of CDs. The mechanism was investigated by addition of EDTA (2 × 10−4 M), which is a competitive chelator, as shown in Fig. S7A, the fluorescence intensity of D-MIP can be increased. Because of this coordination interaction, the electrons in the excited state of CDs will be transferred to the unfilled orbital of metal ions, thus forming PET effect, leading to fluorescence quenching (Chun et al., 2018). When the complex was formed by the coordination between L-PA and Cu2+, there was an absorption band between 490 nm and 654 nm, which means that L-PA can block the PET effect between CDs and Cu2+ same as competitive chelator EDTA, thus leading to the recovery of CDs fluorescence intensity. Fluorescence lifetime can also be used to verify the mechanism of fluorescence quenching and recovery. As shown in Fig. S7B, after the synthesis of D-MIP, the fluorescence lifetime changed from 3.16 to 2.69 ns due to the doping of Cu2+, demonstrating that the PET effect was happened and this was a dynamic quenching process of fluorescence. When L-PA was added, the fluorescence lifetime increased to the original level of 3.14 ns, which showed that L-PA blocked the PET effect and enhanced the fluorescence intensity of D-MIP.

In order to evaluate the selectivity of D-MIP, the fluorescent response of D-MIP to various interferences was studied. As shown in Fig. S8, the response of D-MIP to L-PA is strong, which results in a significant change in fluorescence intensity with higher (F–F0)/F0 than other distractors, proving that the potential substances exerted negligible effects on the D-MIP. Although D-PA is also shown to have a certain selectivity, the selectivity to L-PA is 2.5 times higher than that of D-PA. Hence, the D-MIP based sensor exhibited excellent selectivity to L-PA. In the process of imprinting, a special imprinting cavity is generated that can remember the size, shape and specific chemical function of the template molecule, therefore, D-MIP has a high ability to capture L-PA.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of L-PA in lake water and human urine
ce:section.ce:para: Because of the high sensitivity and selectivity, the sensor based on D-MIP can be directly applied to the detection of L-PA in real samples including lake water and human urine. After the original L-PA of real samples were measured, additional L-PA with different concentrations were added to the samples, and the method proposed in this paper was used for detection. As presented in Table 1, the recoveries of L-PA selective detection in lake water and human urine were in the range from 99.5% to 107.0% and 100.8–107.1%, respectively. Therefore, the D-MIP have a potential to be developed as L-PA sensor with high selectivity, sensitivity and accuracy.

In summary, combining the fluorescence responsive, metal ion affinity, surface imprinting technique and homochiral affinity, integrated D-MIP nanoparticles were synthesized for the specific enantiomeric separation of D/L-PA and detection of L-PA. There were three great advantages of the D-MIP. In the first place, the introduction of copper ion is not only the switch of fluorescence response signal, but also has strong affinity ability with L-PA, which can improve the specific recognition ability of L-PA by directional imprinting. In the second place, D-MIP has wonderful selectivity and reactivity to L-PA, when compared with non-imprinted polymers (D-NIP), due to synergistic multiple binding and the combination of imprinting effects. In the end, the two enantiomers can be distinguished according to the different fluorescence response of D-MIP to L-PA and D-PA and the efficiency of enantiomeric resolution can be improved by the homochiral affinity effect of D-galactose. Due to the excellent chiral recognition and adsorption properties, fluorescence responsive molecularly imprinted polymer nanoparticles may be used as a chiral resolution reagent in the future.

Yan Zhang: Conceptualization, Methodology, Investigation, Data collection, Curation and analysis, Discussion, Validation, Writing - original draft preparation, Writing - review & editing, Formal analysis. Hai-Yan Wang: Discussion, Formal analysis. Xi-Wen He: Discussion, Formal analysis. Yu-Kui Zhang: Discussion, Formal analysis. Wen-You Li: Supervision, Principle investigator, Resources, Funding acquisition, Data curation and analysis, Discussion, Writing - review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Financial support from the National Key Research and Development Program of China (2018YFC1602400) is gratefully acknowledged.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.jhazmat.2021.125249.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
